message:
fix(abi/stdio): fmemopen truncate writes single NUL, not entire buffer; flush NUL-terminates after content

`fmemopen` in write/update modes was overly eager about zeroing the
caller's buffer. Two concrete issues were observable from the ABI tests:

1. `fmemopen(buf, size, "w+")` was calling `std::ptr::write_bytes(buf,
   0, size)` up-front, wiping the caller's buffer immediately on open
   regardless of whether anything was ever written. glibc only NUL-
   terminates at the cursor (i.e. writes a single `\0` at offset 0),
   deferring actual buffer mutation until the caller writes something.

   Fix: tighten the truncate branch in `fmemopen` so it only touches
   the buffer when BOTH `truncate` and `readable` are set AND `size > 0`,
   and in that case write exactly one byte — `*buf = 0` — not `size`
   zeroes. `"w"` (write-only, non-readable) now leaves the caller's
   buffer bytes untouched on open, matching glibc.

2. `sync_fmemopen_full` (called from fseek/fflush/fclose to push the
   internal shadow buffer back out to the caller's buffer) was not
   writing a trailing NUL after the caller-visible content. glibc's
   documented behavior is that on flush, if space remains in the
   caller buffer after the written bytes (and the stream is readable,
   OR something has actually been written), a `\0` is appended at
   `buf[len]` so C string consumers can read the content with standard
   `%s`-shaped APIs.

   Fix: after the `copy_nonoverlapping` that pushes shadow bytes into
   the caller buffer, if `len < info.size` and (`len > 0` or the
   stream is readable), write `*info.buf.add(len) = 0`. This makes
   flushed content always NUL-terminated in the caller buffer.

Test coverage in `crates/frankenlibc-abi/tests/stdio_abi_test.rs`:

- `fmemopen_wplus_truncate_sets_initial_nul_only` replaces the old
  `fmemopen_truncate_clears_caller_buffer` test. Asserts that opening
  `[b'Z'; 8]` with `"w+"` sets exactly one byte (`buf[0] = 0`) and
  leaves indices 1..8 as `Z`, and that fclose doesn't overwrite them.
- `fmemopen_w_truncate_without_write_leaves_caller_buffer_unchanged`:
  `"w"` mode doesn't touch the caller buffer on open (no initial NUL,
  since the stream isn't readable), and fclose without any writes
  also leaves the buffer as-is.
- `fmemopen_w_truncate_defers_caller_buffer_change_until_flush`:
  after a write but before flush, the caller buffer still reads
  `ABCDEFGH`; fflush then pushes `"xy\0DEFGH"` (written bytes +
  flush-time trailing NUL + untouched tail).
- `fmemopen_append_flush_writes_nul_after_appended_content`: opening
  `"ABC\0ZZZZ"` with `"a"` starts the cursor at offset 3 (just past
  the existing NUL), writing `"xy"` and flushing yields
  `"ABCxy\0ZZ"` — the NUL moved to `buf[5]`, not wiped everywhere.

These match the glibc reference behavior observed against the Linux
distro libc on the test host.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
